frameworks.name: Bayesian structure learning
frameworks.one_line: Probabilistic assignment of individuals to groups by combining evidence with prior beliefs.

frameworks.name: dyadic-similarity account
frameworks.one_line: Alternative view that group inference reduces to pairwise similarity between individuals.

frameworks.name: structure learning (cross-domain)
frameworks.one_line: Generalizes structure-learning models from other domains to the social domain.

core_question: How do people infer social group structure from experience with individuals?
key_assumptions: Group structure is latent and must be inferred from observations
Inference combines individual-level evidence with prior beliefs about group structure via Bayes rule
Social-structure learning shares computational principles with non-social structure learning
key_findings: Behavioural patterns in adults and children align with the Bayesian structure-learning framework
Results rule out alternative accounts that reduce group inference to dyadic similarity
interpretations: Social group identification is a structure-learning computation, not mere similarity matching
This framework has implications for intergroup cognition, stereotype updating, and coalition formation
